attributes.stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with light vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal pain for two days. Her last menstrual period was six weeks ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has had one previous uncomplicated pregnancy resulting in a term vaginal delivery three years ago.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is soft with mild suprapubic tenderness. There is no cervical excitation tenderness on vaginal examination.

Investigations reveal:

Beta-HCG: 1000 IU/L two days ago, 850 IU/L today (>25 indicates pregnancy)
Transvaginal ultrasound: Small intrauterine sac measuring 7 x 8 mm. No yolk sac or fetal pole visualised. Normal adnexa. No free fluid in the pelvis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Repeat beta-HCG and ultrasound in 7-10 days
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a pregnancy of unknown location (PUL), with falling beta-HCG levels and an ultrasound showing a possible early intrauterine sac. The absence of concerning features (normal vital signs, minimal pain, no cervical excitation tenderness) and haemodynamic stability make conservative management appropriate.

Repeat beta-HCG and ultrasound in 7-10 days is the most appropriate management. This allows time to determine if this represents a failing early intrauterine pregnancy (most likely given the falling beta-HCG) or early ectopic pregnancy, while monitoring for any clinical deterioration. The majority of PUL cases with falling beta-HCG will resolve spontaneously.

Immediate laparoscopy would be indicated if there were signs suggesting ruptured ectopic pregnancy such as severe pain, haemodynamic instability, or significant free fluid on ultrasound - none of which are present here.

Misoprostol would be inappropriate without definitively confirming the location and non-viability of the pregnancy. While the falling beta-HCG suggests a failing pregnancy, location must be confirmed before active management.

Intramuscular methotrexate would be inappropriate without confirming an ectopic pregnancy. The presence of a possible intrauterine sac makes this particularly contraindicated as it could harm a potentially viable intrauterine pregnancy.

Surgical evacuation of uterus would be premature without confirming the location and definitive non-viability of the pregnancy. This could potentially miss an ectopic pregnancy or terminate a viable pregnancy.

The key to managing PUL is ensuring the patient is stable and informed about warning signs that should prompt immediate medical attention (severe pain, heavy bleeding, dizziness). Serial beta-HCG measurements help determine if the pregnancy is likely failing (falling levels), viable (appropriate rise), or ectopic (suboptimal rise) while location is confirmed. A yolk sac and fetal pole must be visualised to confirm an intrauterine pregnancy. Pseudosac can be seen in up to 20% of ectopic pregnancies. Caution should be applied in interpreting BHCG levels alone as circumstances such as a twin pregnancy with early demise of a twin can produce unusual BHCG levels.
learningPoints: Pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) is defined as a positive pregnancy test without visualisation of an intrauterine or extrauterine pregnancy on transvaginal ultrasound. Yolk sac and fetal pole need to be visualised to confirm an uterine pregnancy.
Serial beta-HCG measurements and repeat ultrasound in 7-10 days is appropriate for stable patients with pregnancy of unknown location.
Clinical stability and absence of concerning features guides the safety of conservative management in PUL.

Visualisation of a intrauterine sac alone does not confirm an intrauterine pregnancy. Yolk sac/fetal pole needs to be seen to confirm a intrauterine pregnancy. Ectopic pregnancies can have a pseudo sac (with no FP or YS) seen in the uterus in up to 20% of cases.
references: Po L, Thomas J, Mills K, Zakhari A, Tulandi T, Shuman M, et al. Management of pregnancy of unknown location and tubal and nontubal ectopic pregnancies. J Obstet Gynaecol Can. 2021;43(5):614-630.e1.
